name: Northern Cyprus
tagline: A de facto Turkish-Cypriot state in the north of a divided island, proclaimed in 1983 and recognised only by Turkey, while the United Nations and the wider world regard the territory as part of the Republic of Cyprus under Turkish occupation.
summary: Northern Cyprus, formally the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus (TRNC), is a self-declared state occupying roughly the northern third of the island of Cyprus. Its emergence is rooted in the island's Ottoman period, which from 1571 established a Turkish-Cypriot Muslim community alongside the Greek-Cypriot majority, in British rule from 1878, and above all in the breakdown of the bicommunal Republic of Cyprus founded in 1960. After intercommunal violence from 1963, a 1974 coup by Greek-Cypriot nationalists backed by the military junta in Athens, and the Turkish military intervention that followed and partitioned the island, Turkish Cypriots formed a separate administration that on 15 November 1983 unilaterally declared independence. The United Nations Security Council, in Resolutions 541 (1983) and 550 (1984), declared that declaration legally invalid and called on states not to recognise it; only Turkey has done so, and the international community continues to regard the area as part of the Republic of Cyprus. The island remains divided by the UN-patrolled Green Line through Nicosia, the failed 2004 Annan Plan referendum the last major reunification attempt, with the dispute unresolved to the present.

founding.label: Declaration of the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus
founding.detail: On 15 November 1983 the Turkish-Cypriot legislative assembly, under the leadership of Rauf Denktaş, issued a unilateral declaration of independence proclaiming the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus in the area held by Turkish forces since 1974. Turkish Cypriots presented the move as the assertion of their right to self-determination and security after years of intercommunal conflict and the failure of negotiations to restore a workable partnership. The United Nations Security Council responded three days later with Resolution 541 (1983), which considered the declaration legally invalid, called for its withdrawal, and called upon all states not to recognise any Cypriot state other than the Republic of Cyprus; Resolution 550 (1984) reaffirmed this position. To date only Turkey has recognised the TRNC, while the United Nations, the European Union, and the international community continue to regard the territory as part of the Republic of Cyprus that is, in their characterisation, under Turkish occupation.

quickFacts.label: International recognition
quickFacts.value: Recognised only by Turkey; the UN regards the area as part of the Republic of Cyprus

quickFacts.label: UN position
quickFacts.value: Security Council Resolutions 541 (1983) and 550 (1984) declared the 1983 declaration legally invalid

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Northern Nicosia (Lefkoşa); Nicosia is a divided capital

quickFacts.label: Communities
quickFacts.value: Turkish-Cypriot (Muslim) majority in the north; Greek-Cypriot majority island-wide

quickFacts.label: Dividing line
quickFacts.value: The UN-patrolled buffer zone (Green Line); UNFICYP deployed since 1964

eras.title: Ancient Cyprus and the Ottoman Inheritance
eras.standfirst: A strategically placed eastern-Mediterranean island ruled in turn by many empires, Cyprus passed to the Ottomans in 1571; their three centuries of rule settled Anatolian Muslims and created the Turkish-Cypriot community that the island's modern history would centre upon.
body: Cyprus, the third-largest island in the Mediterranean, lies at a crossroads between Anatolia, the Levant, and Egypt, a position that made it a prize for successive powers. From Bronze Age city-kingdoms enriched by copper, through Greek settlement, the island passed under Assyrian, Egyptian, Persian, Hellenistic, Roman, and Byzantine rule. Its predominantly Greek-speaking, Orthodox Christian population endured later periods of Frankish (Lusignan) and Venetian control during the medieval and early-modern centuries, leaving a deep Hellenic and Christian character to the island's society and culture.

In 1570 to 1571 an Ottoman army conquered Cyprus from Venice, taking Nicosia and, after a long siege, Famagusta. More than three centuries of Ottoman rule followed. The conquest brought thousands of Muslim settlers, many from Anatolia, and over time a distinct Turkish-Cypriot community took shape alongside the Greek-Cypriot majority. Under the Ottoman millet system the Orthodox Church regained authority over the Greek-Cypriot community, while the two communities, differing in language and religion, lived intermixed across the island in villages and towns.

By the nineteenth century Cyprus held two principal communities whose coexistence would shape its later conflicts: a Greek-Cypriot Christian majority and a Turkish-Cypriot Muslim minority. The Ottoman centuries thus laid the demographic foundation of the island's modern division, establishing the Turkish-Cypriot presence whose security and political status would become central to the formation, much later, of a separate northern entity. Ottoman administration of Cyprus ended in 1878, when, by agreement with Britain, the island's administration was transferred to the British Crown.

events.title: Ottoman conquest of Cyprus
events.summary: An Ottoman army took Cyprus from Venice, capturing Nicosia and Famagusta and beginning more than three centuries of Ottoman rule that settled a Muslim Turkish-Cypriot community on the island.

events.title: Emergence of the Turkish-Cypriot community
events.summary: Muslim settlers, many from Anatolia, established a distinct Turkish-Cypriot community alongside the Greek-Cypriot majority, while the Orthodox Church regained authority over Greek Cypriots under the millet system.

pullquote.text: Three centuries of Ottoman rule settled an Anatolian Muslim population on Cyprus, giving the island the two-community character that its modern history would turn upon.

eras.title: British Rule and the Roots of Conflict
eras.standfirst: Britain administered Cyprus from 1878 and annexed it in the First World War; under colonial rule Greek-Cypriot demands for enosis, union with Greece, and Turkish-Cypriot opposition to it hardened the communal divide that would define the island.
body: By the Cyprus Convention of 1878 the Ottoman sultan agreed that Britain should administer Cyprus while nominal Ottoman sovereignty continued, an arrangement reflecting the island's strategic value near the routes to the Suez Canal. When the Ottoman Empire entered the First World War against Britain in 1914, Britain annexed Cyprus outright; the Republic of Turkey recognised British sovereignty in the Treaty of Lausanne of 1923, and in 1925 Cyprus was declared a Crown colony. The two communities entered the colonial era with sharply differing aspirations for the island's future.

Among many Greek Cypriots the dominant aspiration was enosis, union with Greece, an idea championed by the Orthodox Church and rooted in a sense of Hellenic identity. From 1955 the guerrilla organisation EOKA, led by Colonel Georgios Grivas and supported politically by Archbishop Makarios III, waged an armed campaign against British rule in pursuit of enosis. Turkish Cypriots, fearing absorption into a Greek state in which they would be a vulnerable minority, opposed enosis and increasingly advocated taksim, the partition of the island between the two communities.

Britain, Greece, and Turkey negotiated a settlement at Zürich and London in 1959 that ruled out both enosis and partition, providing instead for an independent, bicommunal republic. Archbishop Makarios and the Turkish-Cypriot leader Dr Fazıl Küçük accepted the compromise on behalf of their communities. The agreements set the terms of independence and bound Greece, Turkey, and Britain as guarantors of the new state's constitutional order, a guarantor role that Turkey would later invoke in 1974.

events.title: British administration of Cyprus begins
events.summary: Under the Cyprus Convention the Ottoman Empire transferred the administration of Cyprus to Britain, which annexed the island in 1914 and made it a Crown colony in 1925.

events.title: EOKA campaign for enosis
events.summary: The guerrilla organisation EOKA, backed by Archbishop Makarios, fought British rule in pursuit of enosis (union with Greece); Turkish Cypriots opposed enosis and called for taksim (partition).

events.title: Zürich and London agreements
events.summary: Britain, Greece, and Turkey, with Greek-Cypriot and Turkish-Cypriot leaders, agreed terms for an independent bicommunal republic, barring both enosis and partition and naming the three powers as guarantors.

pullquote.text: Greek-Cypriot demands for union with Greece and Turkish-Cypriot calls for partition framed the colonial decades and survived into the independent republic.

eras.title: The Bicommunal Republic and Its Breakdown
eras.standfirst: The Republic of Cyprus, born in 1960 with an intricate power-sharing constitution, soon foundered; intercommunal violence from 1963 brought UN peacekeepers in 1964 and drove Turkish Cypriots into armed enclaves, dividing the island in practice a decade before its formal partition.
body: On 16 August 1960 Cyprus became an independent republic under a constitution designed to share power between the two communities. The Greek-Cypriot Makarios III was president and a Turkish Cypriot, Fazıl Küçük, vice-president, each community holding fixed proportions of seats in the legislature, the cabinet, the civil service, and the security forces, with vice-presidential veto rights over key matters. Britain retained sovereignty over two military base areas. The arrangement was intended to protect the Turkish-Cypriot minority, but its rigidity made the new state difficult to govern from the outset.

In November 1963 President Makarios proposed thirteen constitutional amendments that would have reduced Turkish-Cypriot safeguards; the Turkish-Cypriot leadership rejected them as destroying the agreed partnership. Intercommunal violence erupted in December 1963. Turkish Cypriots withdrew or were forced from the organs of the bicommunal state and many gathered into defended enclaves, while the government continued to operate, in practice, as a Greek-Cypriot administration. The two communities increasingly lived apart, and a 'Green Line' was drawn through Nicosia to separate them.

On 4 March 1964 the UN Security Council, by Resolution 186, established the United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus (UNFICYP) to help prevent a recurrence of fighting and restore normal conditions; the force has remained on the island ever since. Further bouts of violence followed in 1964 and 1967, and Turkish Cypriots formed a separate communal administration for the enclaves. By the early 1970s the bicommunal republic of 1960 had effectively ceased to function as designed, even before the events of 1974 partitioned the island geographically.

events.title: Independence of the Republic of Cyprus
events.summary: Cyprus became an independent bicommunal republic under a power-sharing constitution, with Makarios III as president and Fazıl Küçük as Turkish-Cypriot vice-president, and Britain retaining sovereign base areas.

events.title: Breakdown of power-sharing and intercommunal violence
events.summary: After President Makarios proposed constitutional amendments curtailing Turkish-Cypriot safeguards, the partnership collapsed and intercommunal violence broke out; Turkish Cypriots withdrew from the bicommunal state into enclaves.

events.title: UN peacekeeping force (UNFICYP) established
events.summary: By Resolution 186 the UN Security Council created UNFICYP to prevent renewed fighting and restore normal conditions; the force has remained deployed in Cyprus ever since.

pullquote.text: In the interest of preserving international peace and security, to use its best efforts to prevent a recurrence of fighting...
pullquote.attribution: UNFICYP mandate, UN Security Council Resolution 186 (1964)

eras.title: The 1974 Coup and the Partition of the Island
eras.standfirst: A coup by Greek-Cypriot nationalists backed by the junta in Athens overthrew Makarios in July 1974 and gave Turkey the occasion for a military intervention that the Turkish side calls a peace operation and the Republic of Cyprus calls an invasion; the island was divided, and a separate Turkish-Cypriot administration took shape.
body: On 15 July 1974 the Cypriot National Guard, led by Greek officers and acting on orders from the military junta then ruling Greece, staged a coup against President Makarios, who fled the island, and installed Nikos Sampson, a hardline advocate of enosis. The coup alarmed the Turkish-Cypriot community and the government of Turkey, which regarded the prospect of union with Greece as a direct threat. Invoking its status as a guarantor power under the 1960 Treaty of Guarantee, Turkey launched a military operation in Cyprus on 20 July 1974.

Turkish forces landed near Kyrenia and, in a first phase and then a second offensive in mid-August, took control of the northern part of the island, roughly its northern third. The fighting and its aftermath produced large population movements in both directions: most Greek Cypriots fled or were displaced from the north to the south, and most Turkish Cypriots moved from the south to the north, hardening a territorial separation along a ceasefire line. The Greek junta collapsed within days of the intervention, and constitutional government was restored in the south.

The events of 1974 are remembered and named very differently by the parties. The Turkish-Cypriot side and Turkey describe the operation as a peace operation or intervention, a lawful and necessary act to protect the Turkish-Cypriot community and prevent enosis after the coup. The Republic of Cyprus and the international community describe it as an invasion that led to the continuing occupation of the island's north. In February 1975 the Turkish-Cypriot leadership proclaimed the area a 'Turkish Federated State of Cyprus' under Rauf Denktaş, presenting it as the Turkish-Cypriot component of a hoped-for federal settlement rather than a separate sovereign state.

events.title: Coup against Makarios
events.summary: Greek-Cypriot nationalists in the National Guard, backed by the military junta in Athens, overthrew President Makarios in pursuit of enosis and installed Nikos Sampson.

events.title: Turkish military intervention
events.summary: Invoking its guarantor role under the 1960 treaty, Turkey landed forces in Cyprus; described by Turkey as a peace operation and by the Republic of Cyprus as an invasion, it ended with Turkish control of the northern third of the island.

events.title: Turkish Federated State of Cyprus proclaimed
events.summary: The Turkish-Cypriot leadership under Rauf Denktaş proclaimed a 'Turkish Federated State of Cyprus' in the north, presented as a building block for a future federation rather than a separate sovereign state.

figures.name: Makarios III
figures.role: Archbishop and first president of the Republic of Cyprus
figures.blurb: Head of the Orthodox Church of Cyprus and a leader of the enosis movement, Makarios became the first president of the independent republic in 1960; he was overthrown in the 1974 coup but returned to office in the south before his death in 1977.

pullquote.text: What the Turkish side names a peace operation, the Republic of Cyprus and the international community name an invasion and occupation; both characterisations belong to the historical record.
pullquote.attribution: Synthesis of the parties' positions on 1974

eras.title: The Declaration of Independence and Its Rejection
eras.standfirst: On 15 November 1983 the Turkish-Cypriot leadership unilaterally declared the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus; the UN Security Council at once declared the act legally invalid, and only Turkey extended recognition, fixing the island's contested division in its present form.
body: When years of negotiation failed to restore a single working state, the Turkish-Cypriot assembly under Rauf Denktaş moved from federated status to a claim of full sovereignty. On 15 November 1983 it issued a unilateral declaration of independence proclaiming the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus in the area held by Turkish forces. Turkish Cypriots framed the declaration as an exercise of self-determination and a guarantee of security for their community after the violence of 1963 to 1974, while stressing a continued willingness to seek a federal settlement.

The response of the international community was immediate and firm. On 18 November 1983 the UN Security Council adopted Resolution 541, which deplored the declaration, considered it legally invalid, called for its withdrawal, and called upon all states not to recognise any Cypriot state other than the Republic of Cyprus. In 1984 Resolution 550 reaffirmed this stance, condemning further secessionist steps such as the exchange of ambassadors between Turkey and the Turkish-Cypriot leadership, and addressing the fenced-off town of Varosha. Turkey alone recognised the new entity; no other state did so.

From its declaration the TRNC functioned as a de facto state with its own president, parliament, and institutions, Rauf Denktaş serving as its long-time president, but it remained outside the international system, dependent economically and militarily on Turkey and barred from most international organisations and direct trade. The Republic of Cyprus continued to be recognised worldwide as the sole legitimate government of the whole island. Nicosia remained a divided capital, split by the UN buffer zone, and the line between north and south, sealed for many years, separated the two communities almost completely.

events.title: Declaration of the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus
events.summary: The Turkish-Cypriot assembly under Rauf Denktaş unilaterally declared the independent Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus in the island's north, presenting it as an act of self-determination.

events.title: UN Security Council Resolution 541
events.summary: The Security Council deplored the declaration, considered it legally invalid, called for its withdrawal, and called on all states not to recognise any Cypriot state other than the Republic of Cyprus.

events.title: UN Security Council Resolution 550
events.summary: The Security Council reaffirmed Resolution 541, condemned secessionist actions including the exchange of ambassadors with Turkey, and addressed the status of the fenced town of Varosha.

figures.name: Rauf Denktaş
figures.role: Founding president of the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus
figures.blurb: A lawyer and the dominant Turkish-Cypriot political figure for half a century, Denktaş led the community through the conflicts of the 1960s and 1970s, headed the Turkish Federated State from 1975, proclaimed the TRNC in 1983, and served as its president until 2005, championing a two-state vision of the island.

pullquote.text: Considers the declaration referred to above as legally invalid and calls for its withdrawal... Calls upon all States not to recognize any Cypriot State other than the Republic of Cyprus.

eras.title: A Divided Island to the Present
eras.standfirst: Repeated UN-led efforts to reunite the island, above all the Annan Plan put to referendums in 2004, have failed; Cyprus entered the European Union still divided, and the question of Northern Cyprus remains unresolved into the 2020s.
body: Through the 1990s and 2000s the United Nations sponsored successive rounds of negotiation aimed at a bicommunal, bizonal federation. The most far-reaching attempt was the plan associated with UN Secretary-General Kofi Annan, put to simultaneous referendums in both communities on 24 April 2004. A majority of Turkish Cypriots voted in favour, but a large majority of Greek Cypriots voted against, and the plan failed. Days later, on 1 May 2004, the Republic of Cyprus joined the European Union; in legal terms the whole island acceded, but EU law is suspended in the north pending a settlement.

The partial opening of crossing points along the buffer zone from 2003 allowed Cypriots to move between north and south for the first time in decades, easing daily separation without resolving the political dispute. Further negotiations, including talks at Crans-Montana in 2017, failed to produce a settlement, and Turkish-Cypriot leaders increasingly pressed for recognition of a two-state outcome, which the Republic of Cyprus and the United Nations reject in favour of the agreed federal framework. The fenced district of Varosha and the status of property, security, and guarantees remained among the hardest issues.

Into the 2020s the island remains divided. The Republic of Cyprus, a member of the European Union, is recognised internationally as the government of the whole island; the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus continues as a de facto state recognised only by Turkey and, in the view of the United Nations and the wider community, as territory under Turkish occupation. UNFICYP still patrols the buffer zone, its mandate renewed by the Security Council each year, most recently into 2027. The Cyprus question endures as one of the world's longest-running unresolved disputes, its core characterisations still contested between the parties.

events.title: Crossing points open along the Green Line
events.summary: For the first time in decades, crossing points in the UN buffer zone were opened, allowing movement between north and south and easing the communities' separation without settling the dispute.

events.title: Annan Plan referendums fail
events.summary: The UN reunification plan was approved by a majority of Turkish Cypriots but rejected by a large majority of Greek Cypriots in simultaneous referendums, so it was not implemented.

events.title: Republic of Cyprus joins the European Union
events.summary: Cyprus acceded to the EU as a divided island; EU law applies in full only in the south and is suspended in the north pending a political settlement.

events.title: Crans-Montana talks collapse
events.summary: UN-brokered reunification negotiations at Crans-Montana ended without agreement, after which Turkish-Cypriot leaders increasingly pressed for a two-state solution rejected by the Republic of Cyprus and the UN.

pullquote.text: Recognised only by Turkey and regarded by the United Nations as territory under occupation, the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus endures as the unresolved half of a divided island.
pullquote.attribution: Synthesis of the present status of Cyprus

figures.name: Rauf Denktaş
figures.role: Founding president of the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus
figures.blurb: The dominant Turkish-Cypriot leader for half a century, he headed the Turkish Federated State from 1975, proclaimed the TRNC in 1983, and served as its president until 2005.

figures.name: Makarios III
figures.role: Archbishop and first president of the Republic of Cyprus
figures.blurb: Leader of the enosis movement and head of the Orthodox Church of Cyprus, he became the first president in 1960 and was overthrown in the 1974 coup before returning to office in the south.

figures.name: Fazıl Küçük
figures.role: First vice-president of the Republic of Cyprus
figures.blurb: A physician and the principal Turkish-Cypriot political leader at independence, Küçük served as vice-president of the bicommunal republic and accepted the 1959 settlement on behalf of his community.

sources.label: Makarios III - Archbishop, President of Cyprus
Foreign Relations of the United States, 1969-1976, Vol. XXX, Cyprus - intelligence post-mortem on the 1974 coup
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian

sources.label: Cyprus: A Country Study
sources.publisher: U.S. Library of Congress (Federal Research Division)

sources.label: Cyprus - 1963 Constitutional Breakdown (Country Studies)
